Last reviewed: July 18, 2026. This article is general education, not legal, tax, or investment advice. Rules, fees and procedures change - verify everything that matters to your purchase with a Costa Rican attorney and the official sources linked above before acting.

# Costa Rica Property Due-Diligence Checklist for Foreign Buyers

URL: https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-due-diligence-checklist/
Category: buying

**Quick answer:** Costa Rica has a strong central property registry - the [Registro Nacional](https://www.registronacional.go.cr/) - but no title insurance culture like the US, so the buyer's protection is the due-diligence period: typically 15 to 30 days in which your attorney verifies the title, the land, the money owed and the rules that bind the property. This checklist covers what is commonly reviewed. It is general education, not legal advice - the work itself belongs to a Costa Rican real estate attorney.

*This guide explains concepts in plain English for education only. It is not legal advice, and reading it is not a substitute for hiring a Costa Rican real estate attorney. Where this article and your attorney disagree, listen to your attorney.*

## 1. The registry study (estudio registral)

Every titled property has a **folio real** number in the Registro Nacional. From it, your attorney pulls a certified report (certificación literal) showing:

- **Registered owner** - must match your seller exactly; if a corporation owns it, the corporation sells it
- **Property description** - the registered area, boundaries and location
- **Gravámenes (liens)** - mortgages, tax liens, embargoes
- **Anotaciones (annotations)** - legal flags such as pending lawsuits or preventive annotations that can block clean transfer

Anyone can run a first-pass search at the Registro Nacional's portal, but interpreting annotations is attorney work - some are harmless administrative notes, others are deal-stoppers.

## 2. The survey plan (plano catastrado)

Every folio real links to a registered survey plan. Your attorney and, ideally, a licensed surveyor confirm that the plano matches the registry description, and that both match the physical reality on the ground - fences, area and boundaries. Discrepancies between title area and survey area are a classic Costa Rica problem. I cover this document in depth in [Property Title, Survey Plan and Cadastral Map Explained](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-title-plano-catastrado-guide/).

## 3. Municipal taxes and obligations

The municipality where the property sits confirms property taxes (impuesto sobre bienes inmuebles, 0.25% annually of registered value) are current, along with any municipal service charges. Unpaid amounts follow the property, not the seller.

## 4. Land-use certificate (uso de suelo)

Issued by the municipality, the uso de suelo states what the property may legally be used for and its building parameters - density, height, setbacks - under the local zoning plan (plan regulador) where one exists. If your plan is to build, remodel or run a business, this document tells you whether the plan is legal before you pay for the land.

## 5. Water

Confirm how the property is served - AyA, an ASADA, municipal system or registered well - and, for land you intend to build on, whether a **water availability letter** can be obtained, because without one there is no building permit. This deserves its own article: [What Is a Water Letter in Costa Rica Real Estate?](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-water-letter-guide/)

## 6. Road access

Verify the property touches a public road, or that access runs through a properly registered easement (servidumbre) that appears in the registry - not a neighbor's goodwill. Landlocked parcels with informal access are a recurring trap.

## 7. HOA and condominium documents

For property inside a gated community or condo regime: the bylaws, current fee schedule, financial statements, reserves, meeting minutes and any planned special assessments. Rules on rentals, pets, construction and modifications live here.

## 8. Construction permits

For built property: confirm the construction was permitted and matches what was approved (municipal permit records, and for newer builds the CFIA-stamped plans). Unpermitted construction can complicate insurance, remodeling and resale.

## 9. Corporate ownership records

Many properties are held in corporations. If you are buying the shares rather than the asset, due diligence widens dramatically: the corporation's legal books, shareholder registry, tax status and any liabilities all transfer with it. See my guide to [how foreigners buy property in Costa Rica](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/buying-property-costa-rica-foreigners-guide/) for the asset-vs-shares distinction.

## 10. Concession property

If the property sits in the maritime zone (the first 200 meters from the high-tide line), it is likely concession - not titled - land, with its own rulebook: [Titled vs. Concession Beachfront in Costa Rica](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/titled-vs-concession-beachfront-costa-rica/).

## The practical sequence

A typical purchase: signed offer, deposit to a [SUGEF-regulated escrow](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/buying-property-costa-rica-foreigners-guide/), due-diligence period with the checklist above, then closing before a notary who records the transfer in the Registro Nacional. Costs are covered in my [closing costs guide](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-closing-costs-taxes/).

## Sources & Verification

- [Registro Nacional](https://www.registronacional.go.cr/) - titles, liens, survey plans
- [Ministerio de Hacienda](https://www.hacienda.go.cr/) - national tax authority
- [AyA](https://www.aya.go.cr/) - national water authority
- Municipal requirements vary by canton - Santa Cruz and Carrillo cover most of this coast; verify with the specific municipality
- This checklist reflects common practice, not an exhaustive legal standard. Have your own attorney run the process; flag anything unclear for professional review rather than assuming.

## How I help with this

I help organize the real estate side of due diligence: keeping the buyer, seller, attorney, inspector and surveyor aligned, helping collect the documents on this list, arranging property access for inspections, and following up so the 15-to-30-day window is used well. The legal and technical judgments belong to the independent professionals - my job is that nothing sits waiting on a missing paper or an unanswered email.

---

**About this article.** Written for SoldByTiago by [Tiago Leao](https://soldbytiago.com/about.html), a real estate agent with KRAIN Luxury Real Estate in Guanacaste, Costa Rica. Last reviewed: July 18, 2026. This article is general education, not legal, tax, or investment advice. Rules, fees and procedures change - verify everything that matters to your purchase with a Costa Rican attorney and the official sources linked above before acting.


---

# Mar Vista (Flamingo/Brasilito) Real Estate and Community Guide

URL: https://soldbytiago.com/blog/mar-vista-flamingo-real-estate-guide/
Category: communities

**Quick answer:** Mar Vista is a master-planned community on the hillside between Brasilito and Playa Flamingo in Guanacaste. Per [the developer's official site](https://marvistacostarica.com/) and its published materials, the community covers about 750 acres with an emphasis on sustainability, offers homes and building lots across several residential areas, and centers community life on a clubhouse with an infinity pool and the onsite Gracia restaurant - plus something no comparable community on this coast offers: an international school campus at its entrance.

## Location and positioning

Mar Vista occupies elevated land looking over Conchal bay, the Catalinas islands and the Flamingo marina area, with Brasilito's beach town at the bottom of the hill and Playa Flamingo minutes up the road. The elevation is the product: most sections are positioned for ocean or bay views. As with every community guide on this site, check a specific listing's page for computed drive times rather than relying on generalities.

## Residential areas and property types

The community is organized into distinct residential areas mixing finished custom homes, homes under construction and titled building lots - so buyers can enter by building from scratch or purchasing completed construction. Local brokerage materials describe several neighborhoods built around lots averaging roughly 5,000 m² (about 1.25 acres); treat specific lot dimensions as per-listing facts to confirm on the registered survey plan.

## Amenities

Mar Vista's published amenities center on the clubhouse: an infinity-edge pool, the onsite [Gracia restaurant](https://graciacostarica.com/mar-vista/), a fitness center and social areas, plus night-lit tennis, pickleball courts and groomed hiking and biking trails through preserved terrain. The developer's materials emphasize sustainability, including a stated commitment to preserving a substantial share of the acreage as green space - a developer claim worth anchoring in documents if it matters to your purchase.

## The school

[La Paz Community School's Cabo Velas campus](https://soldbytiago.com/school/la-paz-community-school-cabo-velas/) operates at Mar Vista's entrance - the only large community on this coast with an accredited international school at its gate. For relocating families this single fact reshapes the daily logistics of living here. My schools guide profiles La Paz and every other school on this coast.

## Infrastructure and construction considerations

Published materials describe extensive paved internal roads and underground utilities. If you are buying a lot to build:

- Request the current design and construction guidelines and the approval process
- Confirm water service and the availability letter path for your specific lot ([water letter guide](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-water-letter-guide/))
- Confirm electricity availability with the serving utility (this coast is served by ICE or Coopeguanacaste depending on location)
- Verify the lot's registered plano, boundaries and any slope or setback constraints ([title and survey guide](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-title-plano-catastrado-guide/))
- Work the full [land-buying checklist](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/buying-land-guanacaste-zoning-water-building-checklist/) with your attorney and engineer

## HOA information

Current HOA dues, what they cover, and the community's build-out obligations are not comprehensively published; request current figures and the association's financials directly before an offer. Any fee number you see on a listing portal is a snapshot to verify, not a commitment.

## Getting oriented

Distances that matter: Brasilito's restaurants and services at the bottom of the hill, the Flamingo marina and beach minutes north, Playa Conchal a short drive south, and Liberia airport as the air gateway. My agent's-eye comparison of Mar Vista against [Reserva Conchal](https://soldbytiago.com/development/reserva-conchal/) and [Las Ventanas](https://soldbytiago.com/development/las-ventanas-playa-grande/) - plus who I think it genuinely fits - is on my [Mar Vista community page](https://soldbytiago.com/development/mar-vista/).

## Sources & Verification

- [Mar Vista - official site](https://marvistacostarica.com/) - community overview and sustainability positioning
- [Gracia at Mar Vista](https://graciacostarica.com/mar-vista/) - the onsite restaurant
- [La Paz Community School](https://soldbytiago.com/school/la-paz-community-school-cabo-velas/) - the onsite campus, profiled with sources on my schools page
- Acreage, neighborhood structure and amenity details combine developer and brokerage-published materials; HOA figures and construction rules must be confirmed directly with the community.

## What this means for your property search

I help buyers compare Mar Vista's finished homes, builds in progress and lots against each other - and against Reserva Conchal or Las Ventanas nearby - based on how you actually plan to live, school runs included. When a property gets serious, I request the association documents, organize inspections or survey verification with independent professionals, and coordinate through closing.

Last reviewed: July 18, 2026. This article is general education, not legal, tax, or investment advice. # Buying Land in Guanacaste: Zoning, Water, Access and Building Checklist

URL: https://soldbytiago.com/blog/buying-land-guanacaste-zoning-water-building-checklist/
Category: buying

**Quick answer:** Beautiful land is abundant in Guanacaste; *buildable, verifiable, well-papered* land is what you are actually shopping for. Before offering on any lot, work through this checklist: title type, land-use certificate, water, electricity, legal access, topography and soils, setbacks and protection zones, environmental review, the permit path, the survey, and any development rules. Every item below exists because someone skipped it and paid for the lesson.

*This guide explains concepts in plain English for education only. It is not legal advice, and reading it is not a substitute for hiring a Costa Rican real estate attorney. Where this article and your attorney disagree, listen to your attorney.*

## 1. Titled or concession?

First question, always. Most inland Guanacaste lots are fee-simple titled land. Within the maritime zone - the first 200 meters from the high-tide line - land is generally state-owned and held by renewable municipal **concession**, with different rules for foreigners and corporations. Full explainer: [Titled vs. Concession Beachfront](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/titled-vs-concession-beachfront-costa-rica/).

## 2. Land-use certificate (uso de suelo)

Request the municipality's uso de suelo for the lot early. It states permitted use and building parameters - density, coverage, height, setbacks - under the canton's zoning plan (plan regulador) where one exists. Not every canton has a complete, current plan, which itself is information: where zoning is thin, your engineer and attorney carry more of the verification burden.

## 3. Water - the make-or-break item

No valid **water availability letter**, no building permit. Confirm who serves the area (AyA, an ASADA, municipal system or a community's own concession), whether a current letter exists for this exact lot, and what its text actually commits. The differences between a meter, a promise and a permit-grade letter are covered in [What Is a Water Letter?](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-water-letter-guide/) - read it before touring lots, not after.

## 4. Electricity

Guanacaste is served by ICE and, across much of this coast, by the rural cooperative [Coopeguanacaste](https://www.coopeguanacaste.com/en/), which handles new-service availability inquiries through its official channels. Confirm where the nearest connection point is and what extending service to your build site involves - remote-feeling lots can carry very real line-extension costs.

## 5. Legal road access

The lot must touch a public road or hold a properly **registered easement** (servidumbre) that appears in the Registro Nacional. A driveway you can drive today is not the same as access you legally own tomorrow. Landlocked-with-a-handshake is a classic Guanacaste trap.

## 6. Topography

Slope drives buildability and cost: cut-and-fill, retaining, foundations, driveway grade. A steep view lot can cost dramatically more to build on than a flatter one nearby. Walk the land with your architect or engineer before you fall in love with the view.

## 7. Soil and drainage studies

For septic systems - the norm outside a few served areas - your engineer will need soil/percolation information, and sanitary approval is part of the permit chain. Wet-season behavior matters too: a lot that is perfect in February can carry runoff in October. Budget for a soils study on any serious candidate.

## 8. Setbacks and protection zones

Costa Rica's Forestry Law ([Ley 7575](https://pgrweb.go.cr/scij/Busqueda/Normativa/normas/nrm_texto_completo.aspx?nValor1=1&nValor2=41661)) establishes protected zones you cannot build in: commonly cited as 10 meters alongside rivers in flat urban terrain, 15 meters in rural terrain, up to 50 meters on steep slopes, and a 100-meter radius around permanent springs (200 meters where a spring supplies a community). Road setbacks (retiro frontal) come from the zoning rules. Which zones touch your lot is a technical determination for your surveyor and engineer - streams that look like ditches in dry season count.

## 9. Environmental review (SETENA)

Construction projects pass through environmental screening with [SETENA](https://www.setena.go.cr/), the national environmental secretariat; the depth of review depends on the project's category, and low-impact single homes follow lighter paths than developments. Your architect or engineer determines the category and files accordingly - what you need at offer time is awareness that environmentally sensitive features (forest cover, waterways, coastal proximity) can change what is approvable.

## 10. The permit chain

For a home build, expect this sequence: uso de suelo → design by a licensed professional → plan approval through the engineers' and architects' college ([CFIA](https://www.cfia.or.cr/)) via its digital APC system → institutional approvals (water availability, electricity, sanitary) → the municipal construction permit, plus the mandatory INS workers' insurance policy before breaking ground. Timelines vary with complexity; your professional runs this, but your purchase timing should anticipate it.

## 11. Survey verification

Have a licensed surveyor confirm the registered plano catastrado matches the ground - boundaries, area, encroachments. Title-vs-survey discrepancies are common and must be fixed before closing: [how planos and titles work](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-title-plano-catastrado-guide/).

## 12. HOA and development rules

Inside a gated community, the development's design guidelines, build timelines, and fee obligations bind your lot. Get the current documents - not a summary - and read the construction sections before offering. My community guides (for example [Las Ventanas](https://soldbytiago.com/development/las-ventanas-playa-grande/) and [Mar Vista](https://soldbytiago.com/development/mar-vista/)) flag what each development publishes and what you must request.

## Questions to ask before making an offer

1. Is it titled - and does the seller's name match the registry today?
2. What does the uso de suelo permit, exactly?
3. Is there a current, permit-grade water letter for this lot?
4. Where is power, and what does extension cost?
5. Is access public or a registered easement?
6. What do slope, soils and drainage mean for build cost?
7. Which setbacks and protected zones touch the lot?
8. What will SETENA screening require for what I want to build?
9. Does the plano match the ground?
10. What do the development's rules require of me, and by when?

Then run the general [due-diligence checklist](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-due-diligence-checklist/) with your attorney, and if you want a second set of local eyes on a specific lot before you offer - that is literally my job. [WhatsApp me](https://wa.me/50683028660).

## Sources & Verification

- [Registro Nacional](https://www.registronacional.go.cr/) - title and survey records
- [Ley Forestal 7575 (official text)](https://pgrweb.go.cr/scij/Busqueda/Normativa/normas/nrm_texto_completo.aspx?nValor1=1&nValor2=41661) - protection zones
- [SETENA](https://www.setena.go.cr/) - environmental screening · [CFIA](https://www.cfia.or.cr/) - plan approval
- [AyA](https://www.aya.go.cr/) - water authority · [Coopeguanacaste](https://www.coopeguanacaste.com/en/) - electricity on much of this coast
- Setback distances and permit categories involve technical, per-property determinations - the figures above cite the law's commonly referenced values, and your engineer, surveyor and attorney make the call for your lot. Flag anything ambiguous for professional review rather than assuming.

## Applying this to a real lot

Land is where I slow buyers down, in a good way. I help you run this checklist against a specific lot - which questions are already answered, which need the municipality, and which need a professional - then connect the independent surveyor, engineer, water contact and attorney to evaluate what I can't. If you're comparing two or three lots, I'll help you see which one's unknowns are cheapest to resolve.

---

**About this article.** Written for SoldByTiago by [Tiago Leao](https://soldbytiago.com/about.html), a real estate agent with KRAIN Luxury Real Estate in Guanacaste, Costa Rica. Last reviewed: July 18, 2026. This article is general education, not legal, tax, or investment advice. Rules, fees and procedures change - verify everything that matters to your purchase with a Costa Rican attorney and the official sources linked above before acting.


---

# Costa Rica Property Title, Survey Plan and Cadastral Map, Explained

URL: https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-title-plano-catastrado-guide/
Category: buying

**Quick answer:** In Costa Rica, a titled property's legal identity lives in the [Registro Nacional](https://www.registronacional.go.cr/) under a **folio real** number, and its physical shape lives in a registered survey plan called the **plano catastrado**. The two are supposed to match each other and the ground. When they do, transfers are clean; when they do not - and discrepancies are common - you have found the thing your due-diligence period exists to catch.

*This guide explains concepts in plain English for education only. It is not legal advice, and reading it is not a substitute for hiring a Costa Rican real estate attorney. Where this article and your attorney disagree, listen to your attorney.*

## What a property title is here

Costa Rica runs a centralized public registry. Ownership is not proven by a paper deed in a drawer but by what the Registro Nacional's database says today. A notary public (a specially licensed attorney here) records transfers, and the registry entry - not the closing ceremony - is what makes you the owner of record.

## The folio real number

Every titled property carries a folio real: a structured number identifying the province and the property, with a suffix for ownership shares. With it, anyone can pull the property's record. The certified output - the **certificación literal** - shows:

- The registered owner (person or corporation)
- The property's registered area, nature and boundaries
- **Gravámenes** - mortgages, liens, embargoes
- **Anotaciones** - annotations flagging pending legal issues
- The linked plano catastrado number

If a seller cannot produce a folio real, you are probably not looking at titled property at all - it may be possession rights or concession land, which are different games entirely (see [titled vs. concession property](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/titled-vs-concession-beachfront-costa-rica/)).

## The plano catastrado

The plano catastrado is the official survey plan filed with the National Cadastre (part of the Registro Nacional). It shows the property's shape, measured boundaries, area in square meters, orientation, neighbors and access. Every folio real should reference its plano, and a serious purchase cross-checks all three layers:

1. **Registry says** - the area and boundaries in the certificación literal
2. **Plano says** - the drawn, measured survey on file
3. **Ground says** - where the fences, roads and neighbors actually are

## Why title area and survey area can differ

Older properties were often registered from rough descriptions decades before modern surveying; parcels get segregated, planos get redrawn, and registry updates lag. The result: a title stating one area while the plano - or reality - shows another. Small differences are common and manageable; large ones affect value, financing and what you may legally build, and they need resolving *before* closing, typically with an updated survey and registry correction driven by your attorney.

## The surveyor's role

A licensed topographer (topógrafo) physically verifies that the plano matches the ground - corners, boundaries, encroachments, and whether that fence your neighbor built is on their side of the line. For raw land, on this coast, with hills and old fence lines, skipping the survey verification to save a few hundred dollars is a bad trade against a six-figure purchase.

## Documents to request before closing

- Current certificación literal for the folio real (fresh - days old, not months)
- The registered plano catastrado, and confirmation it is the plano linked to the title
- A surveyor's verification that plano and ground agree
- If areas conflict: a written plan from your attorney for resolving the discrepancy pre-closing
- For corporate-held property: the corporation's records tying it to the same folio real

This document set is the backbone of the broader [due-diligence checklist](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-due-diligence-checklist/); if you are buying raw land, continue to the [Guanacaste land checklist](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/buying-land-guanacaste-zoning-water-building-checklist/).

## Sources & Verification

- [Registro Nacional](https://www.registronacional.go.cr/) - the registry and National Cadastre
- [rnpdigital.com](https://www.rnpdigital.com/) - the registry's online consultation portal
- Registry interpretation and discrepancy resolution are attorney and surveyor work - this article explains the documents so you can follow the process, not so you can replace the professionals running it.

## How I help with this

I don't provide legal title opinions or boundary certifications - that is attorney and surveyor work. What I do: help gather the property's available information early, connect you with an independent Costa Rican attorney and licensed surveyor, and keep communication moving during due diligence so a title-versus-plano discrepancy surfaces with time to fix it, not at the closing table.

---

**About this article.** Written for SoldByTiago by [Tiago Leao](https://soldbytiago.com/about.html), a real estate agent with KRAIN Luxury Real Estate in Guanacaste, Costa Rica. Last reviewed: July 18, 2026. This article is general education, not legal, tax, or investment advice. Rules, fees and procedures change - verify everything that matters to your purchase with a Costa Rican attorney and the official sources linked above before acting.

# What Is a Water Letter in Costa Rica Real Estate?

URL: https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-water-letter-guide/
Category: buying

**Quick answer:** A "water letter" - carta de disponibilidad de agua - is an official document from a water provider confirming it has the capacity and infrastructure to serve a specific property. In Costa Rica you generally cannot obtain a construction permit without proving a legal water source, which makes this letter one of the most consequential documents in any land purchase. An existing meter, a promise of future service and a valid availability letter are three different things - and the difference decides whether you can build.

*This guide explains concepts in plain English for education only. It is not legal advice, and reading it is not a substitute for hiring a Costa Rican real estate attorney. Where this article and your attorney disagree, listen to your attorney.*

## Who provides water in Guanacaste

Three kinds of providers dominate:

- **[AyA](https://www.aya.go.cr/)** (Instituto Costarricense de Acueductos y Alcantarillados) - the national water authority, operating systems mostly in urban areas and supervising everyone else
- **ASADAs** - community aqueduct associations that operate rural systems under formal delegation agreements with AyA; they serve roughly a quarter to a third of the country's population through more than 1,800 local systems, and much of the Guanacaste coast falls under one ASADA or another
- **Municipal systems and registered private concessions** - some cantons operate their own aqueducts, and some communities (Las Ventanas de Playa Grande, for example, per its published materials) hold their own water concessions

Which provider serves a property determines who can issue its water letter.

## Why the letter matters

Costa Rican municipalities require proof of a legal water source before issuing a construction permit. No valid availability letter, no permit - regardless of how much you paid for the land. That is why lots are marketed "with water letter" as a selling point, and why a missing or expired letter should change your offer or your plans.

## Not all letters are equal

Providers issue different documents that are easy to confuse:

- A **true availability letter** confirms current, real capacity and infrastructure to serve the property - this is what supports a building permit
- Weaker letters may state only that a system exists nearby, that capacity is *projected*, or that the provider does not object in principle - useful information, but not necessarily permit-grade
- An **existing meter and paid service** prove a connection exists for current use - they do not automatically prove capacity for new construction, a subdivision, or a larger project on the same land

Have your attorney and engineer read the actual text of the letter, not the listing's summary of it.

## Validity - and why timing matters

Availability letters are generally valid for about one year from issuance and can commonly be renewed a limited number of times - renewal is not guaranteed. Guanacaste has real precedent for tightening: AyA has at times restricted specific ASADAs from issuing new-construction letters over capacity or compliance problems (as reported for a group of Nicoya-area ASADAs by [La Voz de Guanacaste](https://vozdeguanacaste.com/en/aya-prohibits-30-asadas-in-nicoya-from-granting-water-permits-for-new-constructions-due-to-irregularities/)). A letter that expires before your permit application can mean starting over under new conditions.

## What buyers should request

- The current availability letter itself - issuer, date, property identification, and exactly what it commits
- If service exists: recent bills and the meter's registration, plus confirmation that capacity extends to your intended construction
- If no letter exists: a written response from the serving provider about availability for the specific lot - before you commit, not after
- For community/private systems: the concession's status and the rules for allocating capacity to owners

## Questions for your team

- To the **municipality**: what water documentation does a building permit require in this canton right now?
- To the **engineer**: does the letter's committed capacity cover the project we intend to design?
- To the **attorney**: is the letter valid, correctly issued for this exact property, and current - and what conditions attach?

## Where this fits

The water letter is item five on my [Costa Rica due-diligence checklist](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/costa-rica-property-due-diligence-checklist/) and a core step in my [Guanacaste land-buying checklist](https://soldbytiago.com/blog/buying-land-guanacaste-zoning-water-building-checklist/). If you are comparing lots anywhere on this coast, ask about water before you ask about the view.

## Sources & Verification

- [AyA - Instituto Costarricense de Acueductos y Alcantarillados](https://www.aya.go.cr/) - national authority; ASADAs operate under its delegation framework
- [La Voz de Guanacaste - AyA restriction on Nicoya ASADAs](https://vozdeguanacaste.com/en/aya-prohibits-30-asadas-in-nicoya-from-granting-water-permits-for-new-constructions-due-to-irregularities/) - reported example of issuance being suspended
- Letter formats, validity and renewal practice vary by provider and change over time - verify the current requirements with the specific provider and the municipality. Nothing here is a determination that any particular property can or cannot be built.

## Need help verifying the next step?

Before recommending land, I help buyers identify whether documented water availability exists for the specific lot - and when it doesn't, I connect the attorney, engineer, or the right municipality or ASADA contact for independent verification. I don't issue or interpret the letter; I make sure the question gets asked before your money is committed, not after.
